raw_consumer_statement: When I first searched for a place to stay in XXXX XXXX, XXXX, I saw a listing similar to the XXXX enclosed which stated XXXX guests and a single nightly fee. Nowhere in the listing or in the subsequent reservation did the host state that the nightly fee was subject to increase depending on the number of guests. If that was her policy, she had a duty to disclose it up front. I used my credit card on XX/XX/XXXX to pay XXXX {$2600.00} for 28 days for XXXX guests. Subsequently, I requested to increase the number of guests to XXXX. XXXX rules state that the original total and the new total should have been shown to me before I submitted my request for the change.. The rules also state that the charge should have been shown before I confirmed the change. That never happened because I relied on the disclosures in the original listing and reservation. Instead, on XX/XX/XXXX, a price adjustment of {$870.00} was charged to my credit card without my knowledge or authorization. At that point, XXXX had {$3500.00} of my money, and a stranger in a foreign country with a no refund cancellation policy had the key to the unit. On XX/XX/XXXX I contacted the host and her response to my request for credit was declined. The matter was then escalated to XXXX XXXX XXXX manned by an international array ( XXXX, XXXX, XXXX, XXXX, XXXX, XXXX XXXX of so-called Support Ambassadors who used tricks like you havent responded ( which was not true ) or sending a message in the middle of the night with a XXXX deadline for reply. That was all a faade to protect the host who provides income to XXXX, regardless of the facts. The whole situation was classic fraud by omission and hidden fees.
